name: Anubis
epithets: He Who is Upon His Mountain
description: Anubis guards the threshold between the living and the dead, weighing hearts against the feather of Ma'at. One of the oldest deities in the Egyptian pantheon, he is patron of embalmers and guide of souls through the Duat. He is both fierce protector and compassionate psychopomp.
historic_data: Worshipped from the Predynastic period. Cult centre at Cynopolis. Extensively referenced in the Pyramid Texts (c. 2400 BCE) and the Book of the Dead.

name: Hecate
description: Hecate presides over magic, witchcraft, and the liminal spaces between worlds. She appears at crossroads at midnight, accompanied by howling dogs and the souls of the restless dead. As torchbearer she illuminates what is hidden; as key-holder she opens all locked doors — both literal and metaphorical.
historic_data: Pre-Olympian origins, possibly Thracian or Anatolian. Major cult centre at Lagina in Caria. Extensively referenced in the Greek Magical Papyri and the Chaldean Oracles.

name: Odin
description: Odin is the relentless seeker of wisdom who sacrificed his eye at Mimir's well and hung nine days on the World Tree to receive the runes. He sends his ravens Huginn and Muninn across the world to gather knowledge and chooses the slain for Valhalla. He is a deity of poetry, magic, war, and death in equal measure.
historic_data: Referenced throughout the Prose Edda and Poetic Edda. Major blót centre at Uppsala in Sweden. Identified with Wotan in Germanic tradition.

name: Brigid
description: Brigid is a triple goddess of fire — the flame of inspiration for the poet, the forge fire of the smith, and the hearth fire of the healer. One of the Tuatha Dé Danann, her festival Imbolc marks the first stirring of spring. She was so beloved she was absorbed into Christianity as Saint Brigid of Kildare.
historic_data: Referenced in Cath Maige Tuired and other Irish mythological texts. Her eternal flame was kept at Kildare by a rotating group of nineteen priestesses. Feast day 1 February, syncretised with Saint Brigid.

name: Lakshmi
description: Lakshmi embodies prosperity in all its forms — material abundance, spiritual wealth, beauty, and grace. She emerged from the churning of the cosmic ocean and chose Vishnu as her eternal consort. She is invoked in every home and business, and her blessings are considered essential for any auspicious beginning.
historic_data: Referenced in the Rigveda (c. 1000 BCE) as Shri. Major worship during Diwali. Her image appears on ancient Mauryan coins.
